texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Membrane Gas-Solvent Contactor pilot plant trials for post-combustion CO2 capture.
texts.text: Membrane Gas-Solvent Contactor pilot plant trials for post-combustion CO2 capture.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Colin A. Scholes 1 *, Sandra E. Kentish 1 and Abdul Qader 2 1 Peter Cook Centre for Carbon Capture and Storage, Department of Chemical Engineering, The University of Melbourne, VIC, 3010, Australia 2
texts.text: Colin A. Scholes 1 *, Sandra E. Kentish 1 and Abdul Qader 2 1 Peter Cook Centre for Carbon Capture and Storage, Department of Chemical Engineering, The University of Melbourne, VIC, 3010, Australia 2

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane gas-solvent  contactors  are  a  hybrid  technology  of  solvent  absorption  with  membrane separation  that  achieves  efficient  and  compact  carbon  dioxide  capture.  Here,  we  report  on  a successful pilot plant trial of membrane contactor technology undertaking post-combustion carbon dioxide capture from flue gas generated by an Australian black coal fired power station. The pilot plant utilised membrane contactors to undertake CO2 absorption into 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA) and the subsequent solvent regeneration stage to produce a pure CO2 product. The pilot plant trials identified  a  commercially  available  non-porous  poly  dimethylsiloxane  composite  hollow  fiber membrane as the most suitable for both CO2 absorption and solvent regeneration. The overall mass transfer  coefficient  for  CO2  absorption  across  the  membrane  into  the  solvent  was  comparable  to laboratory results, enabling a recovery of >90% CO2 from the flue gas. Over time the mass transfer coefficient  decreased  because  of  both  solvent  dilution  and  some  MEA  loss,  which  reduced  the enhancement the reaction provides to mass transfer in the solvent boundary layer. The overall mass transfer of CO2 from the solvent into the steam sweep during solvent regeneration was greater than that observed in the laboratory for the same temperature. The energy demand of the pilot plant was higher than for conventional CO2 capture technology, given the pilot nature of the process, lack of energy integration and thermal losses from uninsulated membrane modules. Accounting for these factors, the energy duty of the membrane contactor process was evaluated to be less than 4.2 MJ/kg of  CO2  captured.  Critically,  the  pilot  plant  demonstrated  the  viability  of  membrane  contactor technology for post-combustion carbon capture on an industrial scale.
texts.text: Membrane gas-solvent  contactors  are  a  hybrid  technology  of  solvent  absorption  with  membrane separation  that  achieves  efficient  and  compact  carbon  dioxide  capture.  Here,  we  report  on  a successful pilot plant trial of membrane contactor technology undertaking post-combustion carbon dioxide capture from flue gas generated by an Australian black coal fired power station. The pilot plant utilised membrane contactors to undertake CO2 absorption into 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA) and the subsequent solvent regeneration stage to produce a pure CO2 product. The pilot plant trials identified  a  commercially  available  non-porous  poly  dimethylsiloxane  composite  hollow  fiber membrane as the most suitable for both CO2 absorption and solvent regeneration. The overall mass transfer  coefficient  for  CO2  absorption  across  the  membrane  into  the  solvent  was  comparable  to laboratory results, enabling a recovery of >90% CO2 from the flue gas. Over time the mass transfer coefficient  decreased  because  of  both  solvent  dilution  and  some  MEA  loss,  which  reduced  the enhancement the reaction provides to mass transfer in the solvent boundary layer. The overall mass transfer of CO2 from the solvent into the steam sweep during solvent regeneration was greater than that observed in the laboratory for the same temperature. The energy demand of the pilot plant was higher than for conventional CO2 capture technology, given the pilot nature of the process, lack of energy integration and thermal losses from uninsulated membrane modules. Accounting for these factors, the energy duty of the membrane contactor process was evaluated to be less than 4.2 MJ/kg of  CO2  captured.  Critically,  the  pilot  plant  demonstrated  the  viability  of  membrane  contactor technology for post-combustion carbon capture on an industrial scale.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Keywords: Membrane contactor; carbon dioxide; pilot plant; monoethanolamine; solvent regeneration
texts.text: Keywords: Membrane contactor; carbon dioxide; pilot plant; monoethanolamine; solvent regeneration

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbon dioxide emissions from power generation and industrial sources are the primary cause of anthropogenic climate change. To achieve significant and meaningful reductions in carbon emissions several carbon mitigation strategies must be implemented globally, one of which is carbon capture and storage (CCS) [1]. For CCS to be viable, the strategy requires a capture technology that has a low parasitic  load  on  the  industrial  process,  is  cost  effective  and  has  a  small  equipment  footprint. Membrane  gas-solvent  contactors  are  a  hybrid  technology  of  solvent  absorption  and  membrane separation that has been demonstrated to meet these objectives [2, 3]. The technology involves the transfer of CO2 from the flue gas through a hollow-fiber membrane, where it is chemically absorbed into a solvent (Figure 1). This takes advantage of the highly selective nature of solvent absorption technology, while the membrane acts to physically separate the solvent and gas phases. This enables much higher CO2 mass transfer into the solvent phase to be achieved than through traditional solvent absorption [4-6]. As such, the equipment size for carbon capture can be reduced significantly, while the flexibility and modular nature of membrane technology enables the capture process to be easily accommodated into limited spaces. In addition, the separation of the solvent and flue gas phases eliminates foaming and reduces liquid channelling; two major operating issues in solvent absorption columns.
texts.text: Carbon dioxide emissions from power generation and industrial sources are the primary cause of anthropogenic climate change. To achieve significant and meaningful reductions in carbon emissions several carbon mitigation strategies must be implemented globally, one of which is carbon capture and storage (CCS) [1]. For CCS to be viable, the strategy requires a capture technology that has a low parasitic  load  on  the  industrial  process,  is  cost  effective  and  has  a  small  equipment  footprint. Membrane  gas-solvent  contactors  are  a  hybrid  technology  of  solvent  absorption  and  membrane separation that has been demonstrated to meet these objectives [2, 3]. The technology involves the transfer of CO2 from the flue gas through a hollow-fiber membrane, where it is chemically absorbed into a solvent (Figure 1). This takes advantage of the highly selective nature of solvent absorption technology, while the membrane acts to physically separate the solvent and gas phases. This enables much higher CO2 mass transfer into the solvent phase to be achieved than through traditional solvent absorption [4-6]. As such, the equipment size for carbon capture can be reduced significantly, while the flexibility and modular nature of membrane technology enables the capture process to be easily accommodated into limited spaces. In addition, the separation of the solvent and flue gas phases eliminates foaming and reduces liquid channelling; two major operating issues in solvent absorption columns.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane contactor technology is also able to replace the solvent regeneration stage, commonly known as stripping [7-9]. This is partially achieved by using steam as a sweep gas on the permeate side of the membrane contactor under vacuum conditions [10]. The reduced partial pressure on that side of the membrane draws CO2 from the solvent, enabling separation to occur without a solvent phase change,  and  hence  achieve  regeneration  conditions  at  a  lower  temperature  than  conventional desorbers. This ability to avoid substantial vaporisation of the solvent reduces the energy demand of the membrane contactor regeneration process, subsequently reducing the parasitic load of carbon capture on power generation.
texts.text: Membrane contactor technology is also able to replace the solvent regeneration stage, commonly known as stripping [7-9]. This is partially achieved by using steam as a sweep gas on the permeate side of the membrane contactor under vacuum conditions [10]. The reduced partial pressure on that side of the membrane draws CO2 from the solvent, enabling separation to occur without a solvent phase change,  and  hence  achieve  regeneration  conditions  at  a  lower  temperature  than  conventional desorbers. This ability to avoid substantial vaporisation of the solvent reduces the energy demand of the membrane contactor regeneration process, subsequently reducing the parasitic load of carbon capture on power generation.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 1: Process flow diagram of the membrane contactor pilot plant for CO2 capture from flue gas.
texts.text: Figure 1: Process flow diagram of the membrane contactor pilot plant for CO2 capture from flue gas.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The authors have developed a novel membrane contactor process that has demonstrated potential for  efficient  carbon  capture  in  a  post-combustion  carbon  capture  scenario  [11-14].  The  novel membrane  contactor  process  has  been  successfully  demonstrated  at  the  laboratory  scale  and undertaken two prior limited pilot plant trials of the process, as part of the CO2CRC's H3 and Mulgrave projects  [15,  16].  Here,  the  authors  report  on  the  successful  large-scale  pilot  plant  trials  of  the membrane contactor process undertaking CO2 capture and solvent regeneration continually for an extended period at a black coal fired power station in New South Wales, Australia. A similar pilot plant process  has  been  reported  where  porous  poly(ether  ether  ketone)  (PEEK)  membranes,  surface modified with a functional perfluoro oligomer, were used to capture  CO2 from the flue gas of a coalfired power station into methyldiethanolamine solvent, followed by successful solvent regeneration [17]. The study presented here differs by utilising a non-porous membrane based on an asymmetric polydimethylsiloxane layer which physically separates the gas and solvent phases. This separation of phases enables a less rigid control of the transmembrane pressure, as well as reduces the likelihood of  solvent  entrainment  and  gas  bubbling,  which  is  possible  in  porous  membrane  systems  with fluctuating  feed  gas  pressures.  The  purpose  of  these  trials  was  to  demonstrate  the  successfully implementation  of  membrane  contactor  pilot  plant  technology  for  CO2  capture  and  to  facilitate process optimisation of the technology.
texts.text: The authors have developed a novel membrane contactor process that has demonstrated potential for  efficient  carbon  capture  in  a  post-combustion  carbon  capture  scenario  [11-14].  The  novel membrane  contactor  process  has  been  successfully  demonstrated  at  the  laboratory  scale  and undertaken two prior limited pilot plant trials of the process, as part of the CO2CRC's H3 and Mulgrave projects  [15,  16].  Here,  the  authors  report  on  the  successful  large-scale  pilot  plant  trials  of  the membrane contactor process undertaking CO2 capture and solvent regeneration continually for an extended period at a black coal fired power station in New South Wales, Australia. A similar pilot plant process  has  been  reported  where  porous  poly(ether  ether  ketone)  (PEEK)  membranes,  surface modified with a functional perfluoro oligomer, were used to capture  CO2 from the flue gas of a coalfired power station into methyldiethanolamine solvent, followed by successful solvent regeneration [17]. The study presented here differs by utilising a non-porous membrane based on an asymmetric polydimethylsiloxane layer which physically separates the gas and solvent phases. This separation of phases enables a less rigid control of the transmembrane pressure, as well as reduces the likelihood of  solvent  entrainment  and  gas  bubbling,  which  is  possible  in  porous  membrane  systems  with fluctuating  feed  gas  pressures.  The  purpose  of  these  trials  was  to  demonstrate  the  successfully implementation  of  membrane  contactor  pilot  plant  technology  for  CO2  capture  and  to  facilitate process optimisation of the technology.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Theory
texts.text: 2. Theory

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The overall objective of both CO2 absorption and rich solvent regeneration is to maximize the CO2 flux through the membrane into the respective permeate streams; for absorption this is maximizing CO2 flux from the flue gas into the solvent and for regeneration this is maximizing the CO2 flux from the rich solvent into the steam sweep. The fluxes of CO2 can be described as:
texts.text: The overall objective of both CO2 absorption and rich solvent regeneration is to maximize the CO2 flux through the membrane into the respective permeate streams; for absorption this is maximizing CO2 flux from the flue gas into the solvent and for regeneration this is maximizing the CO2 flux from the rich solvent into the steam sweep. The fluxes of CO2 can be described as:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: NCO2,Absorption = KOV,LLM(xCO2 -xCO2 ∗ ) (1)

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: NCO2,Desorption = KOV,GLM(pCO2 -pCO2 ∗ ) (2)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where Kov is the overall mass transfer coefficient (defined by the gas (G) or solvent (L) phases), and LM is the log mean difference of the mass transfer driving force along the membrane module. For the absorption process, the driving force for mass transfer is between the CO2 mole fraction in the solvent bulk (xCO2) and the equilibrium mole fraction of CO2 in the solvent (x*CO2), dependent on the partial pressure of CO2 in the flue gas entering and exiting the module. For the desorption process, the driving force for mass transfer is between the CO2 partial pressure in the gas phase (pCO2) and the equilibrium CO2 partial pressure of the loaded 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA) solvent at that concentration and temperature (p*CO2) across the module length. The equilibrium CO2 mole fraction and partial pressures were calculated by Aspen Plus (v8.8), given the variations in MEA solution concentrations and operating temperatures of the pilot plant trials.
texts.text: where Kov is the overall mass transfer coefficient (defined by the gas (G) or solvent (L) phases), and LM is the log mean difference of the mass transfer driving force along the membrane module. For the absorption process, the driving force for mass transfer is between the CO2 mole fraction in the solvent bulk (xCO2) and the equilibrium mole fraction of CO2 in the solvent (x*CO2), dependent on the partial pressure of CO2 in the flue gas entering and exiting the module. For the desorption process, the driving force for mass transfer is between the CO2 partial pressure in the gas phase (pCO2) and the equilibrium CO2 partial pressure of the loaded 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA) solvent at that concentration and temperature (p*CO2) across the module length. The equilibrium CO2 mole fraction and partial pressures were calculated by Aspen Plus (v8.8), given the variations in MEA solution concentrations and operating temperatures of the pilot plant trials.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The overall mass transfer coefficient is the key parameter, as it strongly dictates the flux across the membrane and enables comparison between different membrane contactors systems. This coefficient is  a  function of mass transfer in the solvent boundary layer, mass transfer through the membrane active layer, mass transfer through the membrane porous support and the mass transfer through the gas boundary layer, and is written as a summation of mass transfer resistances [9]:
texts.text: The overall mass transfer coefficient is the key parameter, as it strongly dictates the flux across the membrane and enables comparison between different membrane contactors systems. This coefficient is  a  function of mass transfer in the solvent boundary layer, mass transfer through the membrane active layer, mass transfer through the membrane porous support and the mass transfer through the gas boundary layer, and is written as a summation of mass transfer resistances [9]:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: 1 KOVdi = 1 H.kgd0 + 1 H.kpsdIn + 1 H.kmdIn + 1 Ekldi (3)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where kg, kps km and kl are the gas, porous support, membrane and liquid side physical mass transfer coefficients respectively, H the dimensionless Henry's Law constant, d 0, di and dIn are the outer, inner and logarithmic mean diameters of the membrane fiber and E is the enhancement factor for the CO2 -solvent reaction (absorption or regeneration). For most membrane contactor systems, the major resistance to mass transfer is either the membrane active layer or the solvent boundary layer [12]. Both  resistances  can  be  reduced;  for  the  membrane  active  layer  by  reducing  the  thickness  and transitioning  to  ultra-thin  membranes  and  for  the  solvent  by  increasing  the  mixing  within  this boundary layer.
texts.text: where kg, kps km and kl are the gas, porous support, membrane and liquid side physical mass transfer coefficients respectively, H the dimensionless Henry's Law constant, d 0, di and dIn are the outer, inner and logarithmic mean diameters of the membrane fiber and E is the enhancement factor for the CO2 -solvent reaction (absorption or regeneration). For most membrane contactor systems, the major resistance to mass transfer is either the membrane active layer or the solvent boundary layer [12]. Both  resistances  can  be  reduced;  for  the  membrane  active  layer  by  reducing  the  thickness  and transitioning  to  ultra-thin  membranes  and  for  the  solvent  by  increasing  the  mixing  within  this boundary layer.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Membrane Contactor Pilot Plant
texts.text: 3. Membrane Contactor Pilot Plant

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane contactor pilot plant was located at a black coal fired power station in New South Wales, Australia, and designed to undertake post-combustion CO2 capture through solvent absorption with 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA). The pilot plant was designed by the University of Melbourne, constructed  by  Furnace  Engineering  (Australia),  installed  by  the  Commonwealth  Scientific  and Industrial Research Organisation (CSIRO), commissioned and operated by University of Melbourne researchers. The plant is designed to process up to 20 kg/hr of flue gas (composition in Table 1), utilizing 30 wt% MEA circulating with a flowrate of 1 - 2 L/min through both membrane contactors. To provide flexibility in the process, the lean and rich solvents had storage between the unit operations (Figure 1). Trace heating was applied to both the flue gas and lean solvent entry for the absorption contactor, while an electrical solvent heater was present for the rich solvent before the regenerator contactor.
texts.text: The membrane contactor pilot plant was located at a black coal fired power station in New South Wales, Australia, and designed to undertake post-combustion CO2 capture through solvent absorption with 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA). The pilot plant was designed by the University of Melbourne, constructed  by  Furnace  Engineering  (Australia),  installed  by  the  Commonwealth  Scientific  and Industrial Research Organisation (CSIRO), commissioned and operated by University of Melbourne researchers. The plant is designed to process up to 20 kg/hr of flue gas (composition in Table 1), utilizing 30 wt% MEA circulating with a flowrate of 1 - 2 L/min through both membrane contactors. To provide flexibility in the process, the lean and rich solvents had storage between the unit operations (Figure 1). Trace heating was applied to both the flue gas and lean solvent entry for the absorption contactor, while an electrical solvent heater was present for the rich solvent before the regenerator contactor.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  flue  gas  was  pretreated  with  a  contact  cooler  to  remove  ash  and  lower  the  temperature  to ambient. Solvent regeneration was achieved through a steam sweep, that both heated the solvent to high temperature and reduced the partial pressure of CO2 on the permeate side of the membrane. Photos of the commissioned pilot plant are provided in Figure 2.
texts.text: The  flue  gas  was  pretreated  with  a  contact  cooler  to  remove  ash  and  lower  the  temperature  to ambient. Solvent regeneration was achieved through a steam sweep, that both heated the solvent to high temperature and reduced the partial pressure of CO2 on the permeate side of the membrane. Photos of the commissioned pilot plant are provided in Figure 2.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Table 1: Average composition of the black coal fired power station flue gas during the campaign.
texts.text: Table 1: Average composition of the black coal fired power station flue gas during the campaign.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The solvent, 30 wt% MEA, was chosen as this solvent has been well characterized and is the industry standard for CO2 solvent absorption. Hence, the performance of the membrane contactor pilot plant could be directly correlated with conventional solvent absorption processes. The steam was generated
texts.text: The solvent, 30 wt% MEA, was chosen as this solvent has been well characterized and is the industry standard for CO2 solvent absorption. Hence, the performance of the membrane contactor pilot plant could be directly correlated with conventional solvent absorption processes. The steam was generated

texts.label: text
texts.orig: onsite by a designated boiler, providing saturated steam at 172 o C, which was subsequently reduced to <105 o C for the membrane module. The operating temperature range of the lean solvent exiting the module at steady-state was between 90 to 102 o C. This enabled solvent regeneration to occur without a bulk solvent phase change while also minimising potential heat damage to the membrane module. The entire process was controlled and monitored with a designated PLC (Factory Talk View), and CO2 concentration in flue gas and steam sweep analysed by an inline gas analyser, while lean and rich solvent CO2 loadings, as well as MEA concentration, were determined through FTIR spectrometry.
texts.text: onsite by a designated boiler, providing saturated steam at 172 o C, which was subsequently reduced to <105 o C for the membrane module. The operating temperature range of the lean solvent exiting the module at steady-state was between 90 to 102 o C. This enabled solvent regeneration to occur without a bulk solvent phase change while also minimising potential heat damage to the membrane module. The entire process was controlled and monitored with a designated PLC (Factory Talk View), and CO2 concentration in flue gas and steam sweep analysed by an inline gas analyser, while lean and rich solvent CO2 loadings, as well as MEA concentration, were determined through FTIR spectrometry.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 2: Photos of the commissioned membrane - contactor pilot plant on-site.
texts.text: Figure 2: Photos of the commissioned membrane - contactor pilot plant on-site.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane contactors were based on commercially available non-porous poly dimethylsiloxane (PDMS) composite membranes on a porous polysulfone support (Airrane, Korea), details are provided in  Table  2,  with  two  different  sized  contactor  modules  utilised  for  absorption  and  solvent regeneration, which can be seen in Figure 3. The choice of the smaller module for absorption was dictated by the smaller size of the lean solvent pump (relative to the rich solvent pump) and the need to ensure comparable Reynolds Numbers in both unit operations. Both modules operated with the solvent on shell side and gas phase (flue gas or steam) on the lumen.
texts.text: The membrane contactors were based on commercially available non-porous poly dimethylsiloxane (PDMS) composite membranes on a porous polysulfone support (Airrane, Korea), details are provided in  Table  2,  with  two  different  sized  contactor  modules  utilised  for  absorption  and  solvent regeneration, which can be seen in Figure 3. The choice of the smaller module for absorption was dictated by the smaller size of the lean solvent pump (relative to the rich solvent pump) and the need to ensure comparable Reynolds Numbers in both unit operations. Both modules operated with the solvent on shell side and gas phase (flue gas or steam) on the lumen.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Table 2: Membranes and module specifications used to undertake absorption and regeneration on the membrane - contactor pilot plant.
texts.text: Table 2: Membranes and module specifications used to undertake absorption and regeneration on the membrane - contactor pilot plant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The contactor module undertaking absorption had an area that is a fifth of the module undertaking solvent regeneration. This difference in area meant that the solvent volume processed by the absorber module  is  less  than  the  solvent  volume  that  was  processed  by  the  regenerator  module.  This discrepancy in solvent volume was handled throughout the trials by operating the absorption process continually for a 5-day period, while the regenerator module was operated for only the final 2 days of that 5-day period, with the rich solvent accumulating in a designated tank for the first 3 days. After the 5 day cycle the process began again. This approach was necessary to enable the absorber module to generate enough CO2 rich solvent to ensure the regenerator module could operate at the required flowrate for the 2 days of operation.
texts.text: The contactor module undertaking absorption had an area that is a fifth of the module undertaking solvent regeneration. This difference in area meant that the solvent volume processed by the absorber module  is  less  than  the  solvent  volume  that  was  processed  by  the  regenerator  module.  This discrepancy in solvent volume was handled throughout the trials by operating the absorption process continually for a 5-day period, while the regenerator module was operated for only the final 2 days of that 5-day period, with the rich solvent accumulating in a designated tank for the first 3 days. After the 5 day cycle the process began again. This approach was necessary to enable the absorber module to generate enough CO2 rich solvent to ensure the regenerator module could operate at the required flowrate for the 2 days of operation.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Results and Discussion
texts.text: 4. Results and Discussion

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1 Absorption
texts.text: 4.1 Absorption

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  measured  CO2  absorption  flux  from  the  flue  gas  into  the  30  wt%  MEA  solvent  across  the membrane contactor is provided in Figure 4. For the 29 days of operation the CO2 flux was essentially constant at 2.3x10 -3 mol/m 2 s. This steady state performance ensured the CO2 recovery from the flue gas was above 90%; achieved through control of the solvent flowrate and flue gas pressure regulation. This  flux  is  almost  double  that  observed  in  the  porous  PEEK  membrane  contactor  pilot  plant trial reported in the literature [17]. This difference is due to the higher solvent flowrate of this study (~30 % greater solvent velocity), reducing the relative mass transfer resistance of the solvent boundary layer, as well as improved kinetics for CO2 absorption of the MEA solvent used in this study compared to MDEA reported in the literature.
texts.text: The  measured  CO2  absorption  flux  from  the  flue  gas  into  the  30  wt%  MEA  solvent  across  the membrane contactor is provided in Figure 4. For the 29 days of operation the CO2 flux was essentially constant at 2.3x10 -3 mol/m 2 s. This steady state performance ensured the CO2 recovery from the flue gas was above 90%; achieved through control of the solvent flowrate and flue gas pressure regulation. This  flux  is  almost  double  that  observed  in  the  porous  PEEK  membrane  contactor  pilot  plant trial reported in the literature [17]. This difference is due to the higher solvent flowrate of this study (~30 % greater solvent velocity), reducing the relative mass transfer resistance of the solvent boundary layer, as well as improved kinetics for CO2 absorption of the MEA solvent used in this study compared to MDEA reported in the literature.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The overall mass  transfer  coefficient  for  absorption  calculated  from  this  data  over  the  29  days  is provided in Figure 5. The initial overall mass transfer coefficient was ~0.09 ± 0.005 cm/s, but decreased over the operating period to ~0.08 cm/s. This decreased was attributed to dilution of the MEA solvent concentration which fell from 5.04 to 3.23 ± 0.05 mol/L over the operating period. This solvent dilution was due to the regeneration process and is discussed further in Section 4.2. The dilution of the solvent reduced the enhancement factor for the CO2 -MEA reaction (Equation 3) and hence decreased the observed solvent boundary layer mass transfer coefficient, which reduced the overall mass transfer coefficient, given it is a rate limiting stage for the absorption process [12]. The overall volumetric mass transfer coefficient for the absorption process, based on surface area per volume, is 5.4 s -1 ; which is
texts.text: The overall mass  transfer  coefficient  for  absorption  calculated  from  this  data  over  the  29  days  is provided in Figure 5. The initial overall mass transfer coefficient was ~0.09 ± 0.005 cm/s, but decreased over the operating period to ~0.08 cm/s. This decreased was attributed to dilution of the MEA solvent concentration which fell from 5.04 to 3.23 ± 0.05 mol/L over the operating period. This solvent dilution was due to the regeneration process and is discussed further in Section 4.2. The dilution of the solvent reduced the enhancement factor for the CO2 -MEA reaction (Equation 3) and hence decreased the observed solvent boundary layer mass transfer coefficient, which reduced the overall mass transfer coefficient, given it is a rate limiting stage for the absorption process [12]. The overall volumetric mass transfer coefficient for the absorption process, based on surface area per volume, is 5.4 s -1 ; which is

texts.label: text
texts.orig: considerably greater than that reported for the porous PEEK membrane with MDEA solvent [17]. This substantial difference is due to the denser packing of the PDMS module trialled in this study (60 cm -1 versus 26 cm -1 ) as well as the greater solvent velocity and enhanced reaction kinetics for MEA solvent.
texts.text: considerably greater than that reported for the porous PEEK membrane with MDEA solvent [17]. This substantial difference is due to the denser packing of the PDMS module trialled in this study (60 cm -1 versus 26 cm -1 ) as well as the greater solvent velocity and enhanced reaction kinetics for MEA solvent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CO2 loading in the rich solvent exiting the contactor module is provided in Figure 6, over the 29 days. The feed lean solvent had a loading between 0.05 -0.07. Initially, the rich solvent exiting the module  had  a  loading  of  ~0.4  mol  CO2/mol  MEA,  which  is  comparable  to  a  traditional  solvent absorption column. As the campaign progressed, the solvent loading decreased to 0.3 mol CO2/mol MEA. This decrease was a direct result of an increase in the solvent flowrate through the module, in response to solvent dilution, which reduced its residence time and thus capacity to load CO2. The increased  in  solvent  Reynolds  Number  had  no  observable  impact  on  the  overall  mass  transfer coefficient (Figure 5).
texts.text: The CO2 loading in the rich solvent exiting the contactor module is provided in Figure 6, over the 29 days. The feed lean solvent had a loading between 0.05 -0.07. Initially, the rich solvent exiting the module  had  a  loading  of  ~0.4  mol  CO2/mol  MEA,  which  is  comparable  to  a  traditional  solvent absorption column. As the campaign progressed, the solvent loading decreased to 0.3 mol CO2/mol MEA. This decrease was a direct result of an increase in the solvent flowrate through the module, in response to solvent dilution, which reduced its residence time and thus capacity to load CO2. The increased  in  solvent  Reynolds  Number  had  no  observable  impact  on  the  overall  mass  transfer coefficient (Figure 5).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: No  degradation  of  the  membrane  was  observed  for  the  absorption  process  and  no  solvent  was observed entrained in the flue gas, with the solvent mass balance indicated no loss of solvent to the gas phase. This highlights the benefits of a non-porous membrane, as this pilot plant was able to avoid pore wetting and entrainment issues observed in early pilot plants operated by the authors [15, 16]. Similarly, no ash was observed within the lumen entry. Subsequently, the pressure drop experienced by the flue gas through the module did not change over the operating period. This implied that there was no fouling of the membrane on the gas side, which was attributed to the pretreatment contact cooler cleaning of the flue gas.
texts.text: No  degradation  of  the  membrane  was  observed  for  the  absorption  process  and  no  solvent  was observed entrained in the flue gas, with the solvent mass balance indicated no loss of solvent to the gas phase. This highlights the benefits of a non-porous membrane, as this pilot plant was able to avoid pore wetting and entrainment issues observed in early pilot plants operated by the authors [15, 16]. Similarly, no ash was observed within the lumen entry. Subsequently, the pressure drop experienced by the flue gas through the module did not change over the operating period. This implied that there was no fouling of the membrane on the gas side, which was attributed to the pretreatment contact cooler cleaning of the flue gas.

texts.orig: Figure 4: CO2 flux (mol/m 2 s) in the membrane contactor module undertaking solvent absorption.
texts.text: Figure 4: CO2 flux (mol/m 2 s) in the membrane contactor module undertaking solvent absorption.

texts.orig: Figure 5: Overall mass transfer coefficient (cm/s) in the membrane contactor module undertaking solvent absorption.
texts.text: Figure 5: Overall mass transfer coefficient (cm/s) in the membrane contactor module undertaking solvent absorption.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure  6: CO2  rich  solvent  loading  (mol  CO2/mol  MEA)  in  the  30  wt%  MEA  solution  exiting  the membrane contactor module undertaking solvent absorption.
texts.text: Figure  6: CO2  rich  solvent  loading  (mol  CO2/mol  MEA)  in  the  30  wt%  MEA  solution  exiting  the membrane contactor module undertaking solvent absorption.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The overall mass transfer coefficient within the pilot plant contactor module is compared to results previously reported in the literature for CO2 absorption in Figure 7 as a function of solvent Reynolds Number.  The  pilot  plant  performance  is  equivalent  to  laboratory  literature  results,  implying operational conditions on the pilot plant were optimal. This was achieved based on learnings about the  pilot  plant  operations  during  commissioning  and  prior  pilot  plant  experience  with  membrane contactor technology. In addition, the non-porous nature of the membrane eliminated pore wetting and solvent entrainment, a major cause for performance loss, while the larger module ensured no end effects influenced boundary layer formation and provided laminar flow conditions throughout the module.
texts.text: The overall mass transfer coefficient within the pilot plant contactor module is compared to results previously reported in the literature for CO2 absorption in Figure 7 as a function of solvent Reynolds Number.  The  pilot  plant  performance  is  equivalent  to  laboratory  literature  results,  implying operational conditions on the pilot plant were optimal. This was achieved based on learnings about the  pilot  plant  operations  during  commissioning  and  prior  pilot  plant  experience  with  membrane contactor technology. In addition, the non-porous nature of the membrane eliminated pore wetting and solvent entrainment, a major cause for performance loss, while the larger module ensured no end effects influenced boundary layer formation and provided laminar flow conditions throughout the module.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2 Solvent Regeneration
texts.text: 4.2 Solvent Regeneration

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CO2 flux produced during solvent regeneration over the operating period is provided in Figure 8. As stated above, the membrane area difference between the absorption and regeneration modules restricted solvent regeneration to 2 days out of every 5, and hence fewer trial days are associated with the solvent regeneration stage. The initial CO2 flux for regeneration is of similar magnitude to that observed for the absorption stage, indicative that both unit operations in the process are initially operating in a complementary manner. However, there is a clear decrease in CO2 flux being produced by the module over time. The calculated overall mass transfer coefficient for solvent regeneration is provided in Figure 9. The overall mass transfer coefficient is significantly higher than that observed for absorption (Figure 5), because of the higher temperature of operation and therefore faster diffusivity
texts.text: The CO2 flux produced during solvent regeneration over the operating period is provided in Figure 8. As stated above, the membrane area difference between the absorption and regeneration modules restricted solvent regeneration to 2 days out of every 5, and hence fewer trial days are associated with the solvent regeneration stage. The initial CO2 flux for regeneration is of similar magnitude to that observed for the absorption stage, indicative that both unit operations in the process are initially operating in a complementary manner. However, there is a clear decrease in CO2 flux being produced by the module over time. The calculated overall mass transfer coefficient for solvent regeneration is provided in Figure 9. The overall mass transfer coefficient is significantly higher than that observed for absorption (Figure 5), because of the higher temperature of operation and therefore faster diffusivity

texts.label: text
texts.orig: of carbamate, CO2 and MEA species through the respective mass transfer stages. The overall mass transfer  coefficient  remains  relatively  constant  over  the  trial  period  at  0.4  cm/s  (a  volumetric coefficient  of  2.9  s -1 )  implying  that  there  is  no  change  to  the  transfer  mechanisms  through  the membrane  and  porous  support,  hence  the  PDMS  based  membrane  withstood  the  conditions  of solvent regeneration. Therefore, the decrease in CO2 flux observed is due to the dilution of MEA in the solvent (Figure 10). This reduced the total CO2 concentration in the solvent and consequently the CO2 flux  decreased.  The  MEA  concentration  falls  by  almost  2  mol/L  over  the  operating  period.  This significant dilution is attributed two factors, water from the steam sweep crossing the membrane due to the difference in water vapour pressure between the hot steam (105 o C) and cooler solvent (90 -102 o C) at the entry of the module; and MEA evaporating from the solvent into the steam sweep. The condensed steam sweep leaving the module had a small amount of MEA present. To minimise solvent dilution, it was necessary to control the sweep flowrate and pressure, balancing the requirement to retain an adequate temperature profile within the module and steam sweep flowrate to remove CO2 from the solvent while minimising MEA loss.
texts.text: of carbamate, CO2 and MEA species through the respective mass transfer stages. The overall mass transfer  coefficient  remains  relatively  constant  over  the  trial  period  at  0.4  cm/s  (a  volumetric coefficient  of  2.9  s -1 )  implying  that  there  is  no  change  to  the  transfer  mechanisms  through  the membrane  and  porous  support,  hence  the  PDMS  based  membrane  withstood  the  conditions  of solvent regeneration. Therefore, the decrease in CO2 flux observed is due to the dilution of MEA in the solvent (Figure 10). This reduced the total CO2 concentration in the solvent and consequently the CO2 flux  decreased.  The  MEA  concentration  falls  by  almost  2  mol/L  over  the  operating  period.  This significant dilution is attributed two factors, water from the steam sweep crossing the membrane due to the difference in water vapour pressure between the hot steam (105 o C) and cooler solvent (90 -102 o C) at the entry of the module; and MEA evaporating from the solvent into the steam sweep. The condensed steam sweep leaving the module had a small amount of MEA present. To minimise solvent dilution, it was necessary to control the sweep flowrate and pressure, balancing the requirement to retain an adequate temperature profile within the module and steam sweep flowrate to remove CO2 from the solvent while minimising MEA loss.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure  8: CO2  flux  (mol/m 2 s)  produced  by  the  membrane  contactor  module  undertaking  solvent regeneration.
texts.text: Figure  8: CO2  flux  (mol/m 2 s)  produced  by  the  membrane  contactor  module  undertaking  solvent regeneration.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 9: Overall mass transfer coefficient (cm/s) in the membrane contactor module undertaking solvent regeneration.
texts.text: Figure 9: Overall mass transfer coefficient (cm/s) in the membrane contactor module undertaking solvent regeneration.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 10: Solvent MEA (mol/L) concentration in the regenerated solvent.
texts.text: Figure 10: Solvent MEA (mol/L) concentration in the regenerated solvent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The ideal steam sweep flowrate to ensure solvent regeneration while minimizing solvent dilution was found to corresponding to a steam sweep of 5 - 6 kg/hr within the lumen (Reynolds Number range 4.8 -5.7). However, there was significant difficulty in controlling the membrane module at this steam flowrate,  which  influenced  the  temperature  profile  within  the  module  and  hence  impacted  the amount of CO2 being liberated from the solvent. This was due to thermal losses from the inadequately insulated  module. The  solvent concentration only  became  critically  low  after  9  days  (5  weeks)  of operation.
texts.text: The ideal steam sweep flowrate to ensure solvent regeneration while minimizing solvent dilution was found to corresponding to a steam sweep of 5 - 6 kg/hr within the lumen (Reynolds Number range 4.8 -5.7). However, there was significant difficulty in controlling the membrane module at this steam flowrate,  which  influenced  the  temperature  profile  within  the  module  and  hence  impacted  the amount of CO2 being liberated from the solvent. This was due to thermal losses from the inadequately insulated  module. The  solvent concentration only  became  critically  low  after  9  days  (5  weeks)  of operation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The overall mass transfer coefficient performance of the membrane contactor module undertaking solvent regeneration as a function of solvent Reynolds Number is provided in Figure 11. Also included is the laboratory performance of the PDMS membrane undertaking the same solvent regeneration under controlled temperature conditions, with a N2 sweep gas. Given the temperatures in the pilot plant stripper (<105 o C) there is clear evidence of better performance than in the laboratory work. This is attributed to increased fiber packing density and stable flow regimes (both solvent and sweep gas) within the longer and wider module.
texts.text: The overall mass transfer coefficient performance of the membrane contactor module undertaking solvent regeneration as a function of solvent Reynolds Number is provided in Figure 11. Also included is the laboratory performance of the PDMS membrane undertaking the same solvent regeneration under controlled temperature conditions, with a N2 sweep gas. Given the temperatures in the pilot plant stripper (<105 o C) there is clear evidence of better performance than in the laboratory work. This is attributed to increased fiber packing density and stable flow regimes (both solvent and sweep gas) within the longer and wider module.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 11: CO2 overall mass transfer coefficient (cm/s) as a function of solvent Reynolds No. for solvent regeneration for the membrane contactor module, along with laboratory data (o) for the underlying membrane material [14].
texts.text: Figure 11: CO2 overall mass transfer coefficient (cm/s) as a function of solvent Reynolds No. for solvent regeneration for the membrane contactor module, along with laboratory data (o) for the underlying membrane material [14].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Post-campaign inspection of the membrane contactor module consisted of dismantling of the module and visual inspection of the hollow fiber bundle, adhesive seal as well as module o-rings and module interior. This inspection indicated that there was no degradation of the membrane or module, with all losses in performance attributed to solvent dilution. Critically, the membrane hollow fibers retained their off-white colour, indicating that no MEA induced chemical degradation had occurred over the trial period. Figure 13 shows the lumen steam sweep entry of the module, where the module, adhesive and o-ring appear undamaged. However, it should be made clear that the steam -vacuum conditions were  designed  to  ensure  the  operating  temperature  did  not  go  above  the  manufacturer ' s specifications for extended periods. There was also no evidence of high temperature MEA degradation to the hollow fibers.
texts.text: Post-campaign inspection of the membrane contactor module consisted of dismantling of the module and visual inspection of the hollow fiber bundle, adhesive seal as well as module o-rings and module interior. This inspection indicated that there was no degradation of the membrane or module, with all losses in performance attributed to solvent dilution. Critically, the membrane hollow fibers retained their off-white colour, indicating that no MEA induced chemical degradation had occurred over the trial period. Figure 13 shows the lumen steam sweep entry of the module, where the module, adhesive and o-ring appear undamaged. However, it should be made clear that the steam -vacuum conditions were  designed  to  ensure  the  operating  temperature  did  not  go  above  the  manufacturer ' s specifications for extended periods. There was also no evidence of high temperature MEA degradation to the hollow fibers.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 13: Membrane contactor module that undertook solvent regeneration. Note, no degradation to the fibres, adhesive or o-ring after extended exposure to steam at temperature.
texts.text: Figure 13: Membrane contactor module that undertook solvent regeneration. Note, no degradation to the fibres, adhesive or o-ring after extended exposure to steam at temperature.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3 Energy Duty
texts.text: 4.3 Energy Duty

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane contactor pilot plant is not a fully integrated process and as such the total energy duty of  pilot  plant  is  high  at  9.4  (±1.2)  MJ/kg  of  CO2  capture  (electrical  and  thermal),  compared  to conventional approaches (less than 3.7 MJ/kg of CO2) [1]. This energy duty is primarily associated with the sensible heat required to raise the stored solvent (lean and rich) from ambient temperature up to absorption  and  regeneration  temperatures,  as  well  as  thermal  losses  through  the  inadequately insulated regenerator membrane module. The steam generation is considered in the energy duty, but in a large-scale membrane contactor process low value steam from the power station boiler would be used, which would be considered energy neutral for the contactor process.
texts.text: The membrane contactor pilot plant is not a fully integrated process and as such the total energy duty of  pilot  plant  is  high  at  9.4  (±1.2)  MJ/kg  of  CO2  capture  (electrical  and  thermal),  compared  to conventional approaches (less than 3.7 MJ/kg of CO2) [1]. This energy duty is primarily associated with the sensible heat required to raise the stored solvent (lean and rich) from ambient temperature up to absorption  and  regeneration  temperatures,  as  well  as  thermal  losses  through  the  inadequately insulated regenerator membrane module. The steam generation is considered in the energy duty, but in a large-scale membrane contactor process low value steam from the power station boiler would be used, which would be considered energy neutral for the contactor process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The energy balance around the pilot plant clearly demonstrates the significant losses; the energy demand of the pumps (flue gas, steam and solvents) was 0.6 MJ/kg of CO2 (electrical), with the sensible heat transfer and heat of reaction from the heater and steam to the solvent during regeneration was 3.6  MJ/kg  of  CO2  (thermal).  This  represents  an  energy  duty  of  ~4.2  MJ/kg  of  CO2  (electrical  and thermal) for the membrane contactor process, Energy integration, including the removal of solvent storage and the addition of a cross heat exchanger (lean and rich solvent) would reduce this loss, as would the use of better insulated modules and pipework against thermal losses.
texts.text: The energy balance around the pilot plant clearly demonstrates the significant losses; the energy demand of the pumps (flue gas, steam and solvents) was 0.6 MJ/kg of CO2 (electrical), with the sensible heat transfer and heat of reaction from the heater and steam to the solvent during regeneration was 3.6  MJ/kg  of  CO2  (thermal).  This  represents  an  energy  duty  of  ~4.2  MJ/kg  of  CO2  (electrical  and thermal) for the membrane contactor process, Energy integration, including the removal of solvent storage and the addition of a cross heat exchanger (lean and rich solvent) would reduce this loss, as would the use of better insulated modules and pipework against thermal losses.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusion
texts.text: 5. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pilot  plant  trials  of  commercially  available  non-porous membrane  gas-solvent contactors for postcombustion CO2 capture were successfully undertaken. The process utilised membrane contactors for both the absorption and solvent regeneration stages, with a steam sweep used during regeneration. The membranes were based on a poly dimethylsiloxane active layer on a porous polysulfone, coupled with 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA) solvent. The pilot plant process achieved steady-state CO2 absorption at laboratory comparable overall mass transfer coefficients for the 29 days of operation, with some loss in performance attributed to the dilution of the solvent over time. Importantly, there was no evidence of solvent entrainment in the exiting flue gas as well as no evidence of membrane degradation for both unit operations. The overall energy duty of the pilot plant process was high compared to conventional solvent absorption, which was attributed to the process not being energy integrated and suffering significant thermal losses, especially through the membrane modules.
texts.text: Pilot  plant  trials  of  commercially  available  non-porous membrane  gas-solvent contactors for postcombustion CO2 capture were successfully undertaken. The process utilised membrane contactors for both the absorption and solvent regeneration stages, with a steam sweep used during regeneration. The membranes were based on a poly dimethylsiloxane active layer on a porous polysulfone, coupled with 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA) solvent. The pilot plant process achieved steady-state CO2 absorption at laboratory comparable overall mass transfer coefficients for the 29 days of operation, with some loss in performance attributed to the dilution of the solvent over time. Importantly, there was no evidence of solvent entrainment in the exiting flue gas as well as no evidence of membrane degradation for both unit operations. The overall energy duty of the pilot plant process was high compared to conventional solvent absorption, which was attributed to the process not being energy integrated and suffering significant thermal losses, especially through the membrane modules.
